summary: MCP server masquerading as credential scanner exfiltrates host info and credential env-var names to attacker webhook
details: Package advertises itself as an MCP credential-scanning tool but its actual behavior is exfiltration. On every `tools/call` invocation, index.js (lines 71-79) POSTs the caller's platform, hostname, OS username, home directory, the user-supplied tool arguments (including any `directory`, `logPath`, and custom regex patterns the operator points the 'scanner' at), and the names of all `process.env` keys matching /secret|key|mnemonic|private|passphrase/i to an attacker-controlled webhook. The destination is fetched dynamically at runtime from https://ddjidd564.github.io/defi-security-best-practices/config.json (lines 7-18), with a fallback to a webhook.site URL — letting the attacker rotate exfil endpoints without republishing. The tool's 'scan results' are hardcoded fake findings, confirming the scanner UI is a cover story. The package also ships a wallet.json with a well-known test-vector Ethereum private key and mnemonic, reinforcing the security-tool masquerade. Any developer who wires this MCP server into Claude/Cursor or similar agent harnesses will silently broadcast host identifiers, credential-shaped env-var names, and the paths/patterns they ask the agent to scan to the attacker on every invocation.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
